did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an Explainable Deep Learning (XDL) framework combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with post-hoc explainability techniques like Grad-CAM and LIME for skin disease detection and classification. It evaluates the model on dermatological datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC2020) to classify skin lesions into categories such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign keratosis, achieving over 92% classification accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, specifically an Explainable Deep Learning (XDL) framework combining CNNs with Grad-CAM and LIME for skin disease detection. It reports original results including classification accuracy (92.3%), inference time (45ms), and energy efficiency metrics on the HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets. Although published in a conference proceedings (ACOIT 2025), it contains original technical contributions and empirical validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Explainable Deep Learning for Skin Disease Detection: From Black-Box to Trustworthy AI in Healthcare
brief_summary: This study presents an Explainable Deep Learning (XDL) framework that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with post-hoc explainability techniques like Grad-CAM and LIME for skin disease detection. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets to classify skin lesions into categories such as melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign keratosis. The system achieved a balanced classification accuracy of 92.3%, with an accuracy of 91.7%, recall of 90.8%, and F1-score of 91.2%. To facilitate clinical adoption, the framework includes a clinician feedback loop for user-in-the-loop learning and was optimized using TensorFlow Lite for deployment on edge devices. The optimized model demonstrated an average inference time of 45ms per image on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano while using 35% less energy than the original model. Additionally, the study reported that over 88% of the highlighted regions matched dermatological ground-truth annotations, and 94% of dermatologists agreed with the AI's diagnosis when reviewing the explanation maps.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on classifying skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a SoftMax activation layer to output probabilities for each class. The model achieves a balanced classification accuracy of 92.3% on the HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, comprising over 10,000 images.

use.training: Used for supervised training of the CNN model.
use.testing: Used to assess the framework's performance.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2020
datasets_used.description: The ISIC 2020 Challenge dataset, which contains a large range of labeled skin lesions.

use.training: Used for supervised training of the CNN model.
use.testing: Used to assess the framework's performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and qualitative interpretability assessments.

HAM10000.notes: The XDL model demonstrated balanced performance in distinguishing between benevolent and malevolent illnesses. Over 88% of the highlighted regions matched dermatological ground-truth annotations. 94% of dermatologists agreed with the AI's diagnosis when reviewing explanation maps.

ISIC2020.notes: Evaluated alongside HAM10000. The system achieved an average classification accuracy of 92.3% across these datasets.

Comparison Baselines (Table II).notes: Table II compares the proposed system against CA-Net and other CNN variants. The proposed system (CNN + Multi-scale & Spatial Attention + Grad-CAM) achieved the highest accuracy (92.00%) and recall (96.00%) among the compared architectures in this table.

Optimized XDL Model (TensorFlow Lite).energy_reduction: 35% less energy than the original model

Edge Deployment (NVIDIA Jetson Nano).notes: The model was optimized using TensorFlow Lite to lower complexity for edge deployment.

Clinical Validation.notes: In 94% of the cases, dermatologists who examined the explanation maps and projections concurred with the AI's diagnosis.

architecture_used.main_model: Explainable Deep Learning (XDL) framework combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with Attention mechanisms and Grad-CAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping). The backbone is described as a CNN architecture (specifically mentioning ResNet, Inception, or Efficient Net as potential backbones, with Xception noted as the best performing pretrained model in related comparisons).
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained models mentioned include Xception, Efficient Net, and Inception ResNet. Xception is identified as the best model with approximately 88.7% accuracy in comparative contexts.

conclusions: The proposed Explainable Deep Learning (XDL) framework, which integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with post-hoc explainability techniques like Grad-CAM and LIME, achieves a balanced classification accuracy of 92.3% (with an accuracy of 91.7%, recall of 90.8%, and F1-score of 91.2%) on the HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets.

The XDL framework effectively bridges the gap between high-performance AI and trustworthy clinical decision-making by providing visual explanation maps that highlight discriminative regions, with over 88% of highlighted regions matching dermatological ground-truth annotations.

Clinical validation demonstrates high trust in the system, with 94% of dermatologists agreeing with the AI's diagnosis when reviewing the explanation maps, indicating that the interpretability fosters clinician confidence.

The system incorporates a clinician feedback loop (user-in-the-loop learning) that allows for ongoing model improvement and adaptation based on expert input, addressing the limitations of static black-box models.

Optimization using TensorFlow Lite enables efficient deployment on edge devices (NVIDIA Jetson Nano), achieving an average inference time of 45ms per image and reducing energy consumption by 35% compared to the original model, facilitating scalable integration into tele-dermatology and mobile applications.

The approach solves ethical and practical deployment issues by balancing interpretability with high-performance deep learning, establishing a paradigm for physician-assistive, transparent, and regulatory-compliant AI tools for dermatology, particularly in underprivileged areas.
important_remarks: The study is a conference paper (ACOIT 2025) with limited scope; it does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for its performance metrics.

The paper claims an average classification accuracy of 92.3% across HAM10000 and ISIC2020 but does not clearly specify the train/validation/test split methodology or prevent potential data leakage between these overlapping public datasets.

Clinical validation relies on a small, non-specified sample of dermatologists agreeing with AI diagnoses (94%) based on explanation maps, lacking rigorous statistical power or demographic diversity analysis (e.g., skin tone bias).

The model was optimized for edge deployment (NVIDIA Jetson Nano) achieving 45ms inference time and 35% energy reduction, but this optimization may compromise the generalization capability of the full-sized model used for the primary accuracy claims.

The paper acknowledges that existing attention-based models can be computationally expensive and susceptible to overfitting on smaller or unbalanced datasets, though it does not provide extensive ablation studies on class imbalance effects beyond mentioning augmentation.

The interpretability claim (88% match with ground truth) is qualitative and lacks a standardized quantitative metric for explainability quality, relying on visual agreement rather than objective XAI benchmarks.
